title: Fractional shot noise of an SU(N) Kondo system
abstract: We consider transport through a multi-level interacting quantum dot (N-QD) in Kondo regime. Using the Kotliar-Ruckentein slave boson approach (SBMFA) for N-level Anderson model we define effectively noninteracting quasiparticles of the SU(N) Kondo system ($N=2,3,4,5,6$). Kondo resonance transmission coefficients determine linear noise describing quasiparticle partitioning. To discuss nonlinear conductance, susceptibilities and shot noise in the strong coupling regime, we apply Fermi liquid theory with parameters expressed by susceptibilities of pseudofermions determined within SBMFA. Nonlinear shot noise is dominated by two-quasiparticle scattering. However, we demonstrate that for occupation regions distant from the electron-hole symmetry point, the role of three-body correlations must be revealed.
summary: This paper theoretically analyzes the transport properties, especially nonlinear shot noise, in an SU(N) Kondo system using a sophisticated combination of Slave Boson Mean Field Theory and Fermi Liquid theory. It shows how higher-order correlation functions, beyond simple two-body terms, become essential for accurately describing current fluctuations away from equilibrium. The results advance the understanding of correlated electron physics in quantum dots by predicting measurable fractional noise signatures.
main_problem: The paper investigates transport properties, including nonlinear conductance and fractional shot noise, in an SU(N) Kondo system operating in the strong coupling regime.
main_result: It demonstrates that while linear noise is captured by mean-field approaches, accurate description of nonlinear noise requires incorporating three-body correlation functions when far from electron-hole symmetry.
method: The analysis combines the Kotliar-Ruckentein slave boson approach (SBMFA) with Fermi Liquid Theory (FLT) to calculate higher-order corrections beyond mean-field approximations.
model_or_system: The system is a multi-level interacting quantum dot (N-QD) modeled by an N-level Anderson Hamiltonian, analyzed for SU(N) symmetries up to N=6.
key_observables: Linear and nonlinear conductance, susceptibilities ($\chi^{(2)}, \chi^{[3]}$), and fractional shot noise ($S$).
important_parameters: Kondo temperature ($T_K$), degeneracy $N$, bias voltage ($V$), and Coulomb interaction strength ($U$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on extending the SBMFA by incorporating Fermi liquid corrections, which is necessary because the basic SBMFA neglects crucial fluctuations.
figures_summary:
Figures illustrate slave boson amplitudes and fluctuation parameters ($\delta E_
u$) as a function of bias voltage ($V$), and show how generalized Wilson ratios characterize correlation strengths.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the SBMFA framework for linear response, to applying FLT corrections for nonlinear transport, culminating in explicit formulas for shot noise dependent on higher-order correlations.
why_interesting: This work provides advanced theoretical tools (FLT extensions to slave bosons) to calculate non-equilibrium quantum noise signatures that are highly sensitive indicators of strong electronic correlations and symmetry structure.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Kondo effect with even symmetry SU(N=2,4,6): (a,c,d) linear and nonlinear Fano fac- tor F0(K), single-channel quantum conductance Gν and Wilson ratio Wνν′ −1 as a function of dot energy Ed for N = 2, 4, 6. (b) Gate-dependent FK compared with nonlinear current and shot-noise, rescaled by the square of Kondo temperature. Inset shows a numeri- cal and analytical approximation of TK (blue and red dashed lines) (U = 3, Γ = 0.025, T = 0, energies are given in the units W/50). Dark cyan curve in the inset of (c) presents TK for Γ = 0.05.
Figure 2: Two and three body correlators rescaled by TK for SU(4) and SU(3) Kondo state (blue and magenta lines). The parameters used in calculations are the same as in Figure 1.

Figure 3: Odd SU(3) and SU(5) Kondo effect: (a,b) Gate-dependent F0(K), Gν and Wνν′ −1. Insets show a numerical and analytical approximation of TK (blue and red dashed lines) (U = 3, Γ = 0.025, T = 0).

Figure 4(b) compares the dependencies of non- linear Fano factor on the rank of SU(N) group for the selected occupancies. FK > 1 indicates the dominance of bunching processes. This oc- curs for Q = N/2. In the systems with a larger number of electrons, the ranges in which quasi- particle grouping processes dominate (FK > 1) shift to higher degenerations (N) (Figure 4(b)). Figure 5: (a,b) Density plots of gate-dependent total Fano factor |F| as function of bias voltage V for SU(4) and SU(3) Kondo states (U = 3, Γ = 0.025, T = 0). ±1 indicates the sign of the Fano factor.
